doc.id: Potkay et al. - 2021 - Coupled whole-tree optimality and xylem hydraulics explain dynamic biomass partitioning.pdf
doc.title: Coupled whole-tree optimality and xylem hydraulics explain dynamic biomass partitioning

model_card.name: Potkay2021_Supplement_S4_StemConductanceScaling
model_card.section: S.4 Scaling of Stem Conductances with Tree Size
task: Derive whole-tree maximum stem conductance (k_S) scaling with tree size
Account for sapwood vs heartwood via heartwood–sapwood transition diameter (D_t)

assumptions.text: Stem conductance is represented as a series of conductors connected in series axially and in parallel radially; total conductance is computed by integrating inverse conductivity along height.

evidence.snippet: Stem conductance is represented as a series of conductors connected in-parallel radially and connected in-series axially ... (kS)−1 = ∫0H (KS(x))−1 dx (S.4.1).

assumptions.text: Local xylem conductivity KS(x) is approximated by (i) Hagen–Poiseuille scaling integrated radially across conduits and (ii) multiplied by (H/x)^2 to account for in-parallel flow across multiple branches at distance x from the apex.
Sapwood area fraction is conserved along the tree height, expressed as dt(x)/d(x) = Dt/D (uniform heartwood fraction along stem).

evidence.snippet: We further assume that sapwood area is conserved along the height of the tree (dt(x)/d(x) = Dt/D).

assumptions.text: Branch outer diameter scales approximately linearly with distance from apex: d(x) ∝ x (at least for the Scots Pine parameterization used for validation).

evidence.snippet: branch diameter approximately scales linearly with distance from apex, d(x) ∝ x ...

assumptions.text: Tree height and basal diameter are linked via a power-law allometry captured as log(H) ∝ c0·log(D), enabling substitution of H with D in conductance scaling.

evidence.snippet: recall log(H) ∝ c0·log(D) ... replacement of H with its equivalent D ...

equations.explanation: Whole-tree maximum stem conductance (kS) computed as axial series integral of inverse local conductivity KS(x) from apex (x=0) to canopy height (x=H).

Local xylem conductivity at distance x from apex based on Hagen–Poiseuille scaling integrated radially from heartwood–sapwood transition radius to outer radius, and multiplied by an in-parallel branching term (H/x)^2.
Replacement of the Rx^4Nx·r proportionality by a single power-law in r with dummy scaling exponent β.
Whole-tree maximum stem conductance scaling after combining the axial integral with the sapwood-conservation and diameter-scaling assumptions.
Further simplification by substituting H using the height–diameter allometry log(H) ∝ c0·log(D).
Final stem conductance function used by THORP: replaces dummy exponent β with c2/c0, introduces proportionality constant b2 and reference diameter Dref for dimensional consistency.
Derivative of kS with respect to basal diameter D (used later for allocation/optimality derivatives).

variables.definition: Maximum stem xylem conductance at the whole-tree scale.

evidence.snippet: The scaling of stem xylem conductance, kS [mol H2O·s−1·MPa−1] ...

variables.definition: Xylem conductivity at distance x from the apex.

evidence.snippet: KS(x) is the xylem conductivity [mol H2O·m·s−1·MPa−1] at a distance x [m] from the apex.

variables.definition: Distance from apex along the stem/branch system (integration coordinate in S.4).

evidence.snippet: ... at a distance x [m] from the apex.

variables.definition: Tree height (canopy height) used as upper limit of the axial integration and in branching multiplier (H/x)^2.
Outer branch/stem diameter at distance x from the apex.

evidence.snippet: outer diameter, d(x) [m], for a branch at a distance x from the apex.

variables.definition: Diameter of the heartwood–sapwood transition at distance x from the apex.

evidence.snippet: heartwood–sapwood transition of the branch, dt(x) [m] ...

variables.definition: Basal (trunk) diameter at the base of the tree, with d(H)=D.

evidence.snippet: Note that d(H) = D ...

variables.definition: Basal diameter of the heartwood–sapwood transition at the base of the tree, with dt(H)=Dt.

evidence.snippet: ... and dt(H) = Dt, where D and Dt are the trunk diameter and diameter of the heartwood-sapwood transition at the base ...

variables.definition: Xylem conduit radius as a function of radial coordinate r.

evidence.snippet: Rx is the xylem conduit radius ...

variables.definition: Number of xylem conduits (as a function of r) used in local conductivity scaling.

evidence.snippet: ... and Nx is the number of xylem conduits.

parameters.name: eta
parameters.role: Dummy scaling exponent replacing the Rx^4Nx·r proportionality with r^β.

evidence.snippet: We replace the Rx^4Nx r proportionality with a single power-law function of r with dummy scaling-exponent, β ...

parameters.name: c_0
parameters.role: Height–diameter allometry exponent in log(H) ∝ c0·log(D).

evidence.snippet: We fixed c0 at 0.64 ... (regressed when plotting log(H) against log(D) ...).

parameters.notes: Value (0.64) is the Scots Pine parameterization used in the paper’s calibration/validation context.

parameters.name: c_2
parameters.role: Scaling exponent describing how kS scales with basal diameter when no heartwood is present; used via β = c2/c0.

evidence.snippet: We replace the dummy exponent, β, with c2/c0, where c2 is a new scaling exponent that describes how stem conductance scales with basal diameter when there is no heartwood ...
We fixed c2 so that kS ∝ H^1.45 for Scots Pine ... i.e. c2 = 1.45·c0.

parameters.notes: The paper sets c2 = 1.45·c0 for Scots Pine (no heartwood case).

parameters.name: b_2
parameters.role: Lumped proportionality constant in the final kS expression (Eq. S.4.7).

evidence.snippet: adding a lumped proportionality constant, b2 [mol H2O·s−1·MPa−1] ...
We estimated b2 for Scots Pine by fitting Eq. S.4.7 to whole-tree stem conductance data ... by ordinary-least squares regression ...

parameters.name: D_{ref}
parameters.role: Reference diameter used for dimensional consistency in Eq. S.4.7.

evidence.snippet: ... and a reference diameter, Dref = 1 m, for dimensional consistency.

steps: Compute dkS/dD = [c2 + (a - c2) * h] / (1 - h) * (kS/D). (Eq. S.4.8)

evidence.snippet: Derivative of maximum stem conductance ... (S.4.8).

notes.text: Eq. S.4.1–S.4.6 are given as proportionalities (∝); Eq. S.4.7 introduces b2 and Dref to convert to an equality used in the model.

evidence.snippet: We also remove the proportionality by adding a lumped proportionality constant, b2 ... and a reference diameter, Dref = 1 m ...

notes.text: The paper’s Scots Pine calibration choices include fixing c0=0.64 and choosing c2 such that kS ∝ H^1.45 when no heartwood is present; b2 is then estimated by OLS fitting of Eq. S.4.7.

evidence.snippet: We fixed c0 at 0.64 ... We fixed c2 so that kS ∝ H^1.45 ... We estimated b2 ... by ordinary-least squares regression ...

chunk_plan.title: S.4 Scaling of Stem Conductances with Tree Size
chunk_plan.rationale: Defines the stem conductance scaling law kS(D,Dt) used in THORP, including the heartwood correction and the derivative dkS/dD needed for allocation/optimality derivatives.

chunk_plan.title: S.5 Light Interception
chunk_plan.rationale: Introduces the light interception model (Beer–Lambert style) and its derivatives used later for allocation fractions.
